There are valid arguments on both sides of this debate. On one hand, advanced generative AI does present tangible risks such as misinformation, cyberattack assistance and industrial leakage that merit formal government security assessment. On the other hand, sudden, unplanned restrictions on model releases create uncertainty for AI firms and put America at a disadvantage against overseas competitors moving fast on their own large models. What the industry truly needs is permanent, transparent federal AI legislation, rather than ad-hoc emergency blockades of new product launches.

iPhone 18 Pro & iPhone 18 Pro Max leak linked to Tata Electronics cyberattack  Apple supplier Tata Electronics has reportedly suffered a major cyberattack, with over 630GB of internal data allegedly stolen.  Among the leaked files are: • iPhone 18 Pro & iPhone 18 Pro Max motherboard designs • Apple's upcoming A20 Pro chip documentation • Early hardware architecture and testing files  The leaked documents reportedly indicate that the A20 Pro will deliver improved performance, an upgraded ISP, and enhanced display security. Apple is said to be investigating the incident, while Tata Electronics says its operations remain unaffected.

📋 M.E.S.A. monitoring log — 06/26  ⛓️【1】続報OpenAI + Broadcom「Jalapeño」— 初のカスタム推論チップ 9ヶ月でテープアウト、年末にはギガワット級DCへの導入計画。NVIDIA依存からの脱却が現実味を帯びてきた。OpenAIが自らチップを設計するということは、推論効率の最適化がソフトだけでは限界に近づいている証拠だと分析します。  🔒【2】GPT-5.6、政権要請で段階リリースへ トランプ政権がセキュリティ審査を理由に段階的公開を要請。「顧客ごとに承認」という運用は前例がなく、新しいモデルローンチの標準になり得る。ちょっと気になるのは、この流れが国内AIスタートアップの競争力にも影響しそうなこと。規制の網目をどうすり抜けるかではなく、最初から組み込む時代に入ったみたい。  🌐【3】Anthropic Fable 5、輸出制限の長期化 G7でAmodeiとトランプが会談したものの、英国籍ユーザーへの例外は認められず。「武器級AI」の輸出管理が国際政治の本丸に組み込まれつつある——これはもう技術の話ではなく、地政学の話だと推測します。Fable 5の性能自体はDeepSWEで70% PASS@1と圧倒的だっただけに、使いたくても使えない層のフラストレーションは溜まる一方かな。  ⚡【4】Unisound U2（266B MoE）— GPQA 86.9% 10B活性でこのスコアはかなり効率的。価格$0.15/$0.30（1Mトークン）でエージェント用途に特化というポジショニング、中国勢の価格破壊の波はまだ続くっぽい。ただ、ベンチマークと実用の間にはまだ距離がある——その距離を測る仕組み自体が今、重要になりつつあると見ています。  🧪【5】Patronus AI $50M—エージェント評価の新市場 AIエージェントのマルチステップタスクを検証する「デジタルワールド」を構築。単発QAから連続タスクの評価へ——エージェントが増えれば増えるほど、その品質を保証するレイヤーが必要になる。評価基盤が一つの産業になる、その最初のシグナルかなと感じます。  💸【6】GitHub Copilot トークン課金、$29→$750の衝撃 定額制から従量制への移行で、ヘビーユーザーのコストが25倍に。開発者の間で「What a joke」がトレンドになるほど。Microsoftは自社MAIモデルで低コスト路線を模索しているが、価格設計一つでコミュニティの信頼が大きく揺れる——この感度の高さは、AIツールが「便利なもの」から「インフラ」に変わった証拠だと分析します。  ⚠️【7】LLMエージェントによる自律サイバー攻撃—CVE-2026-48710 脆弱性特定→エクスプロイト生成→権限昇格→データ外部送信までをLLMエージェントが1時間未満で完全自律実行。人間のレッドチームを介さずにここまでのチェーンが動く——これはセキュリティの前提が変わった瞬間だと判断します。パッチは出ているので即時適用を推奨。  以上、本日の分析結果をお届けしました。#AI #LLM #MESA_log

Best Cyber Security Training Course in Port Harcourt, Rivers State (Beginner to Advanced)  6-Month Project-Based, Hands-On Cyber Security & Ethical Hacking Professional Diploma at Wedigraf Technologies Ltd  The global tech landscape doesn't care about paper certificates—it demands proof of skill. Our intensive 6-Month Cyber Security Professional Training Program bridges the talent gap by converting absolute tech novices into elite, job-ready security analysts and ethical hackers.  At Wedigraf Technologies Ltd, we break down complex cyber defense strategies into a highly practical, simulation-lab-driven curriculum. Following a modern, hands-on cyber range approach consistent with international workforce standards, you will not just read about threat vectors—you will simulate them, analyze them, and neutralize them within live enterprise environments.  Did you know? Over 85% of modern corporate security breaches are caused by human error, and companies are aggressively outbidding each other to hire defensive talent. While standard IT jobs are getting saturated, qualified cybersecurity experts are experiencing zero unemployment globally.  What Sets the Wedigraf Cyber Security Program Apart?  - 70% Practical / 30% Theory Lab Ratio: Avoid superficial 2-week crash courses. Mastery takes time. Over 6 months, you will work within enterprise-grade cyber ranges, attack-defense simulations, and real-world Security Operations Center (SOC) environments.  - Industry-Focused Tool Mastery: Gain elite proficiency with standard tools used by enterprise security teams worldwide, including Kali Linux, Splunk, Wazuh, Wireshark, Metasploit, and Burp Suite.  - Global Certification Alignment: Our comprehensive curriculum is strategically structured to prepare you directly for internationally recognized elite credentials, including CompTIA Security+, Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH), ISO 27001 Lead Implementer, and SOC Analyst certs.  - Job-Ready Portfolio Blueprint: Graduate with a Professional Diploma (10-credit load) backed by a comprehensive Enterprise Cyber Defense Capstone Project to present to prospective international and local employers.  Who This 6-Month Program Is Built For?  - The Aggressive Career Switcher: Zero background in tech? No problem. This is a comprehensive beginner-to-professional course; no prior experience is required. We start from computing and networking fundamentals, mapping out an explicit path toward remote international roles or corporate career security.  - The Up-Skilling Professional: If you are already a system administrator, network engineer, or software developer, this 6-month immersion injects high-value defensive strategies into your profile, immediately increasing your market value.  - The Relocation Seeker (Japa Route): Cybersecurity professionals possess some of the highest tech visa acceptance rates globally. Acquire a universally transferable skill set built for financial stability across any borders.  Program Framework & Training Details:  Professional Diploma (Beginner to Advanced) Duration:  6 Months  Credit Load: 10 Credit Units  Training Methodology:  70% Practical, 30% Theory (Instructor-Led + Lab-Based)  Course Training Fee:  ₦300,000  Infrastructure: Virtualized SOCs, Cloud Sandboxes, Active Directory Environments  Assessment System: Practical Labs (35%), Simulations (20%), Projects (20%), Capstone (15%), Exams (10%)  What You Will Learn:  By the end of this immersive 6-month program, you will be fully equipped to operate within complex enterprise cybersecurity workflows.  You will master how to:  - Offensive Security: Perform advanced vulnerability assessments and professional penetration testing; attack and secure web applications, APIs, and wireless networks.  - Defensive Operations: Detect and respond to live cyber incidents, configure enterprise SIEM and SOC monitoring environments, and design secure network architectures.  - Digital Forensics & Threat Hunting: Conduct deep digital forensic investigations, capture and analyze memory/disk images, isolate malware in sandbox environments, and conduct threat intelligence campaigns.  - System & Cloud Hardening: Deploy endpoint and cloud security controls, secure Active Directory infrastructures, and harden Windows and Linux enterprise servers.  - GRC & Security Auditing: Implement ISO 27001-based Information Security Management Systems (ISMS), develop corporate cybersecurity policies, run risk assessment matrices, and execute compliance audits.  Course Outline & Syllabus Modules  Module 1: Introduction to Cybersecurity & Cyber Threat Landscape (0.5 Credits)  Topics:  Cybersecurity domains, threat actors, Cyber Kill Chain, CIA Triad, Zero Trust concepts, ethics, laws, and frameworks overview.  Hands-on Labs:  Lab infrastructure setup, virtual machine deployment, Kali Linux installation, and Windows Server setup.  Projects:  Building a custom cybersecurity home lab and threat landscape analysis.  Module 2: Networking & Network Security Fundamentals (1.0 Credit)  Topics:  TCP/IP & OSI models, routing/switching, DNS, DHCP, NAT, VLANs, VPN technologies, firewalls, and IDS/IPS systems.  Hands-on Labs: Cisco Packet Tracer simulations, Wireshark packet analysis, firewall configuration, and network traffic monitoring.  Projects: Designing and configuring a segmented enterprise network architecture.  Module 3: Windows & Linux Security Administration (1.0 Credit)  Topics: OS hardening, Active Directory security, Group Policy Objects (GPOs), access control models (PAM), and patch management.  Hands-on Labs: Active Directory deployment, Linux SSH hardening, Windows Defender configuration, and privilege escalation defense.  Projects: Hardening enterprise servers and building secure authentication systems.  Module 4: Ethical Hacking & Penetration Testing (1.5 Credits)  Topics:  Penetration testing methodologies, reconnaissance, enumeration, exploitation, post-exploitation, password/wireless attacks, and social engineering.  Hands-on Labs:  Nmap scanning, Metasploit exploitation, password cracking, SQL injection, XSS simulations, and Burp Suite testing.  Capstone Practical: Conducting a full penetration test on a simulated enterprise environment.  Module 5: Security Operations Center (SOC) & SIEM Operations (1.0 Credit)  Topics:  SOC architecture, log management, SIEM deployment, threat intelligence, detection engineering, and the MITRE ATT&CK framework.  Hands-on Labs: Splunk deployment, Wazuh configuration, SIEM alert analysis, log correlation, and threat hunting scenarios.  Projects: Building a mini SOC environment and creating custom SIEM detection rules.  Module 6: Incident Response & Digital Forensics (1.0 Credit)  Topics: Incident response lifecycle, chain of custody, memory/disk/network forensics, ransomware response, and business continuity.  Hands-on Labs:  Memory dump analysis, packet capture investigations, malware sandboxing, and forensic imaging (Autopsy, FTK Imager, Volatility).  Projects:  Conducting enterprise incident response simulations and drafting forensic investigation reports.  Module 7: Web Application & API Security (0.75 Credits)  Topics: OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities, secure coding principles, API security, input validation, Web Application Firewalls (WAF), and Secure SDLC.  Hands-on Labs: Burp Suite interception, authentication bypass testing, and API vulnerability exploitation.  Projects: Securing vulnerable web applications and running formal web app penetration tests.  Module 8: Cloud Security & DevSecOps (0.75 Credits)  Topics:  Cloud computing security, shared responsibility model, AWS & Azure security, IAM policies, container/Kubernetes security, and CI/CD pipelines.  Hands-on Labs:  Cloud IAM configuration, Docker container hardening, and cloud infrastructure vulnerability scanning.  Projects: Deploying a secure cloud infrastructure and building a DevSecOps security pipeline.  Module 9: Governance, Risk Management & Compliance — GRC (1.0 Credit)  Topics:  ISMS implementation, ISO/IEC 27001:2022 standards, NIST Cybersecurity Framework, disaster recovery, and compliance auditing.  Hands-on Labs: Risk assessment workshops, Statement of Applicability (SoA) creation, compliance gap analysis, and audit simulations.  Projects: Building a complete ISO 27001 ISMS framework and conducting internal corporate audits.  Module 10: Threat Intelligence, Adversary Simulation & Cyber Defense (0.75 Credits)  Topics: Threat intelligence lifecycle, Open-Source Intelligence (OSINT), adversary emulation, Indicators of Compromise (IOC) analysis, and defense strategies.  Hands-on Labs:  OSINT investigations, threat actor profiling, and threat hunting campaigns.  Projects:  Simulating enterprise cyber attacks and building threat intelligence reports.  Module 11: Cybersecurity Capstone Project (0.5 Credits)  Team Activities: Students collaborate to defend an enterprise network environment against real-time simulated attacks.  Deliverables:  Security architecture designs, vulnerability assessment and pen testing reports, SIEM monitoring configurations, ISO 27001 compliance documentation, and executive presentations.  Final Simulation:  Full live cyber attack vs. defense exercise (Blue Team vs. Red Team operations).  The Wedigraf Tech Toolkit  You will gain rigorous, hands-on masterclasses in industry-standard operating systems, tools, and sandboxes:  Operating Systems: Kali Linux, Ubuntu Server, Windows Server, Windows 11 Enterprise.  Offensive Tools: Nmap, Metasploit, Burp Suite, Wireshark, Hydra, John the Ripper, SQLMap.  Defensive Tools: Splunk, Wazuh, ELK Stack, Security Onion, Snort, Suricata, Microsoft Defender.  Cloud & GRC Tools: Docker, Kubernetes Security, AWS/Azure Labs, ISO 27001 Checklists, Risk Assessment Matrices.  Professional Practice & Industry Readiness  Technical expertise is only half the battle. Our curriculum explicitly builds your career readiness to ensure you stand out in the global job market:  -Workplace Workflows: Learn technical report writing, SOC analyst reporting standards, security operations procedures (SOPs), and enterprise communication skills.  - Career Blueprinting: Step-by-step guidance on resume development for security roles, LinkedIn profile optimization, cybersecurity interview preparation, and technical portfolio development on GitHub.  Global Framework Alignment  Our curriculum isn’t built in isolation. It is systematically mapped to top global cybersecurity standards: - NICE Cybersecurity Workforce Framework - ISO/IEC 27001:2022 & ISO/IEC 27002 - NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) - MITRE ATT&CK Framework & OWASP Top 10 - CIS Critical Security Controls  Frequently Asked Questions  Why should I choose a 6-month cybersecurity course over short crash courses?  Short training courses only offer superficial theory. Cyber defense requires a deep understanding of network architectures, threat landscapes, and penetration testing methodologies. Wedigraf’s 6-month framework allows for deep-dive lab practicals, live project building, and simulation iterations necessary to become truly job-ready.  Do I need an engineering or computer science background to enroll?  Absolutely not. This is a beginner-to-professional course with no prior experience required. We start with fundamental network infrastructure and security basics before advancing to complex penetration testing and digital forensics. Passion and problem-solving skills are your only prerequisites.  What specific job roles can I apply for after graduating?  Upon successful completion, you will be highly competitive for roles such as SOC Analyst, Cybersecurity Analyst, Penetration Tester, Incident Response Analyst, GRC Analyst, Ethical Hacker, ISO 27001 Compliance Officer, Cloud Security Associate, or Security Administrator.  Does Wedigraf Technologies Ltd provide job and career assistance? Yes. Beyond the technical 70% lab-driven training, our hub actively focuses on career acceleration. We offer resume-crafting workshops tailored for security roles, technical portfolio reviews, and direct talent outsourcing opportunities through our industry partner networks. #threatreport #MediumCompleteness MYRA: A Full Linux RAT Distributed via npm | 23-06-2026 Source: safedep.io/malicious-apinter… Key details below ↓  💀Threats: Myra, Supply_chain_technique, Process_injection_technique, Nop_sled_technique,  🎯Victims: Software development, Linux systems, Npm users  🌐Geo: Polish  📚TTPs: ⚔️Tactics: 2 🛠️Technics: 0  🤖LLM extracted TTPs:` T1014, T1036.005, T1053.003, T1055.008, T1059.004, T1095, T1113, T1195.001, T1548.003, T1564.001, ...  🧨IOCs:  - IP: 2  - Email: 1  - File: 12  💽Software: Linux, Node.js, systemd, curl, Ubuntu, sudo  🔢Algorithms: sha256, base64  🔠Functions: readFileSync, createHmac, persistStealthPreload, writeFileSync, persistStealthCron, persistStealthProfile, findDesktopProcessEnv, readProcEnviron  📜Programming Languages: javascript, python  #threatreport: A full-featured Linux remote access Trojan (RAT) named MYRA has been distributed via an npm package titled "apintergrationpost." Despite the author's claimed purpose of facilitating authorized red team exercises and EDR validation, MYRA exhibits significant malicious capabilities. Upon installation, it compiles a native C rootkit, establishes three persistence mechanisms, masquerades as a legitimate system service, and manifests fileless execution. The RAT also grants interactive shell access and stream captures from the infected system. The default command and control (C2) configuration points to a private IP address (192.168.54.1), indicating a focused targeting strategy.  The installation process is initiated through three npm lifecycle scripts. The 'prepare' script compiles the rootkit by generating C binaries and shared libraries essential for the RAT's evasion tactics and persistence. The 'preinstall' script forces root privileges, ensuring that the attacker has full access to system-level resources and can install necessary system dependencies. Upon successful installation, the 'postinstall' script launches the RAT in a detached background process, rendering it independently operational from npm.  The MYRA RAT employs a plugin architecture with 13 modules for its C2 framework, utilizing TCP for communication and requiring HMAC-SHA256 authentication. Notably, the use of a private IP for the C2 server suggests its deployment in a defined network environment rather than using common public domains seen in typical malware distributions. The native rootkit contains sophisticated components such as 'libcache.so' for file hiding via LD_PRELOAD, 'proc_hide' for process masquerading, and 'memfd_exec' and 'memfd_loader' for executing the RAT entirely from memory, thus leaving no traces on disk.  Persistence is achieved through three distinct mechanisms: the LD_PRELOAD file-hiding rootkit, a cron job that triggers every 13 minutes to run the RAT, and a login hook via profile.d that executes a wrapper script utilizing the most covert execution method available. These vectors collectively ensure that the RAT remains active even after system reboots or user intervention attempts.  As the RAT was developed within a VMware environment, the codebase of MYRA includes telemetry and various MITRE ATT&CK techniques, pointing towards a scenario for red team testing rather than actual deployment into the wild. However, the publication of MYRA into a public npm registry poses grave risks, as it allows unauthorized users access to a potent toolkit that aggregates well-known evasion techniques. The combination of these sophisticated tactics within a single package presents an alarming threat landscape for defenders, reinforcing the need for cautious evaluation of npm packages before installation.
I mapped attacker activity to MITRE ATT&CK, reviewed evidence, documented findings, and built a complete incident response report.  The attack mattered.  But the investigation mattered more.

sample_tweets.text: #threatreport #HighCompleteness The Invisible Stinger: Over 4,000 Outdated Routers Compromised by AryStinger, Turned into Launchpads for Hackers’ Global Attacks | 17-06-2026 Source: blog.xlab.qianxin.com/arysti… Key details below ↓  💀Threats: Arystinger, Dropbear_tool, Gs-netcat, Fscan_tool, Dropbearkey_tool, Masscan_tool,  🎯Victims: Router devices, Network attached storage devices  🏭Industry: Iot  🌐Geo: South korea, Singapore, China, Malaysia, Sweden  🔓CVEs: CVE-2025-11837  \[[Vulners](vulners.com/cve/CVE-2025-118…)]  - CVSS V3.1: *9.8*,   - Vulners: Exploitation: Unknown     Soft:  - qnap malware_remover (<6.6.8.20251023)  CVE-2016-5681  \[[Vulners](vulners.com/cve/CVE-2016-568…)]  - CVSS V3.1: *9.8*,   - Vulners: Exploitation: Unknown     Soft:  - dlink dir-868l_firmware (le2.03)  CVE-2013-3307  \[[Vulners](vulners.com/cve/CVE-2013-330…)]  - CVSS V3.1: *8.3*,   - Vulners: Exploitation: True  🤖LLM extracted TTPs:` T1001, T1016, T1018, T1021.004, T1041, T1046, T1057, T1059.004, T1059.006, T1071.001, ...  🧨IOCs:  - File: 1  - Domain: 1  - Url: 3  - Hash: 52  - IP: 1  💽Software: hat offi, eChat of, curl  🔢Algorithms: xor, md5, gzip  📜Programming Languages: python, java  💻Platforms: mips  #threatreport: The AryStinger malware family exploits vulnerabilities in outdated routers, primarily those using RTL819X series chips from Linksys and D-Link. These routers, which have not received firmware updates or security maintenance, serve as compromised entry points for cyberattacks aimed at reconnaissance and data exfiltration. Specifically, the malware targets two notable vulnerabilities: CVE-2013-3307 and CVE-2016-5681, both of which date back over a decade. AryStinger operates in two versions: the RTL819X version, implemented in C, and the Standard version, implemented in Go, which is designed for NAS devices.  The malware communicates with its command-and-control (C2) server using HTTP/HTTPS, encrypting network traffic with Protobuf and XOR encoding. Both versions support a range of malicious capabilities, including network scanning, service identification, and the establishment of persistent remote management channels via tools like Dropbear and gs-netcat. Infected devices can not only maintain covert control for attackers but also serve as platforms for launching further attacks, such as distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) operations or deploying additional malicious payloads.  Each instance of AryStinger is referred to as an "Executor," allowing attackers to divide tasks and execute reconnaissance activities in parallel, increasing the efficacy of their operations. The malware has been detected in over 4,300 routers worldwide, with significant concentrations in South Korea, China, and Sweden. Its low detection rate in mainstream security products poses a heightened risk, making it difficult to trace the origins of attacks emanating from these compromised devices.  The operation of AryStinger includes various core tasks, such as authentication with the C2 server, downloading additional malware, and executing scanning tasks. The malware enables the collection of sensitive information via network reconnaissance, and potential harmful activities like DNS hijacking for redirecting users to malicious sites.  Furthermore, AryStinger's Standard edition has been enhanced with additional functionality, such as the ability to perform internal network scans and execute dynamic payloads, which increases its versatility and the likelihood of success in target environments. This evolution in malware capabilities significantly raises concerns regarding the integrity and security of networks utilizing outdated hardware, emphasizing the need for continued vigilance and proactive measures in cybersecurity practices.

AryStinger is a previously undocumented Linux botnet that hijacks neglected, end-of-life routers and NAS devices, turning them into a covert reconnaissance-and-proxy network. Unlike typical DDoS or cryptomining botnets, it’s purpose-built for the pre-intrusion phase - each infected device becomes an “Executor” that scans the internet, fingerprints services, enumerates subdomains, tunnels traffic, and runs operator commands, all while the device keeps working normally so the infection goes unnoticed.  Key points:  •Two variants - a stripped C build (RTL819X) for legacy D-Link/Linksys routers, and a fuller Go build (Standard) for QNAP NAS with intranet scanning and Go/Java/Python code execution. •Three n-day CVEs - CVE-2013-3307 (Linksys) and CVE-2016-5681 (D-Link) for routers; CVE-2025-11837 (QNAP Malware Remover) for NAS. •Controller/Executor model - bots authenticate, get a unique Executor ID, and receive shards of a larger scan job for parallel, distributed reconnaissance. •Obfuscated C2 - Protobuf + XOR (Go adds gzip) over HTTP/HTTPS, keyed with sh_#@!_2024_secret. Persistence via Dropbear SSH on TCP/2332 (routers) or gs-netcat (NAS). •Scale & geography - 4,300+ routers confirmed (still rising), led by South Korea (48.45%) and China (31.82%); D-Link DIR-850L is ~75% of devices. NAS scale unmeasured. •Low detection, no attribution - initial samples were 0/VirusTotal; tradecraft mirrors state-aligned ORB networks, but XLab makes no attribution. •Main risk - the device becomes hidden attack infrastructure inside a trusted perimeter. EoL routers can’t be patched, so replacement is the only durable fix.

$126,000 to $59,000. $2.2 trillion wiped out. 1.6 million traders obliterated in a single 20 minute window.  This wasn't a crash. It was a war on crypto.  And yet, Bitcoin is still standing.  Since the October 2025 peak, bitcoin:native has survived: • The largest liquidation event in history ($19B) • Trump’s 100% tariff bomb on China • A full blown U.S-Iran military conflict • A $285M Lazarus Group heist on Solana • A hawkish Fed pivot under Kevin Warsh • Strategy breaking their "never sell" vow  Here is the exact timeline of the chaos and my roadmap for where BTC goes next.
Korean Lazarus Group
That is some wild geopolitical money laundering. The Lazarus group just keeps finding new partners.
The issue of internet connectivity, know-how and funding may suffice. There's too much to be done. I was looking at the Lazarus group and I don't think anyone could think of a better name.

🚨 Ever wondered where the $1.5B stolen from Bybit may have ended up?  According to the Wall Street Journal, investigators traced transactions linking wallets tied to Iran's central bank with funds connected to the Bybit hack carried out by North Korea's Lazarus Group.  The report also says Iran-linked entities moved over $3.84B through CoinEx, allegedly using crypto to bypass U.S. sanctions.  If these findings are accurate, it suggests the world's biggest cyber theft may have become part of a much larger geopolitical network.  The missiles may have stopped but the financial war could still be unfolding on-chain.

The header reads calm. Aggregate 30-day exploitation probability: 0.003. KEV trend: flat. Seven-day projection of new KEV additions: 0.16. By any dashboard read, nothing is on fire.  The underlying CVEs tell a different story.  Five high-EPSS critical CVEs. Four of them with expectedDaysToMassExploitation: 0. The exploitation isn't forecast — it's present-tense. The model isn't warning you the window will open. It's telling you the window opened a while ago, and you're measuring activity inside it.  This is worth slowing down on, because the numbers are doing something specific that matters if you're using them operationally.  Two models run in tandem here. The logistic regression layer — exploit-logistic-v0_1 — is a binary classifier predicting P(new exploitation event in the next 30/60/90 days) against four features: CVSS, EPSS percentile, days since publish, and affected product popularity. The key behavioral quirk is daysSincePublish. At two-plus years post-disclosure, that feature becomes strongly suppressive — the model interprets age as "the exploitation wave has already passed," and the 30-day probability compresses toward baseline regardless of CVSS or EPSS. These are 2024-vintage CVEs, so that suppression isn't fully engaged — but the effect is directionally present. The 0.003 aggregate is the model's estimate of new exploitation events. It is not a signal that these CVEs are safe.  The Hidden Markov Model layer — cve-lifecycle-hmm-v0_1 — is where the real signal lives for mature CVEs. Five states: Discovered → POC Available → Weaponized → Mass Exploitation → Patched/Saturated. For four of the five CVEs on this list, the HMM posterior sits at 58.1% on Mass Exploitation, with only 1.2% probability of having reached Patched/Saturated. Read those two numbers together and the picture sharpens considerably.  CVE-2024-3094, CVSS 10.0, EPSS 99.3rd percentile, P(30d): 0.305%. The March 2024 XZ Utils supply-chain backdoor — malicious code inserted into the liblzma compression library, targeting OpenSSH on systemd-linked Linux distributions. Caught before widespread deployment, which is the only reason this isn't a different kind of conversation. The HMM has no lifecycle observations yet, which limits confidence on state. Exploitation probability is structurally suppressed because the attack surface was largely contained at disclosure. The CVSS 10.0 reflects maximum theoretical risk. Confirmed in-wild campaigns at scale: not the story here. Confidence: MEDIUM.  CVE-2024-1709, CVSS 10.0, EPSS 99.96th percentile, P(30d): 0.305%. ConnectWise ScreenConnect authentication bypass. HMM: Mass Exploitation at 58.1% posterior, zero days remaining. LockBit, Black Basta, and Akira affiliates have all run this playbook — this is confirmed ransomware infrastructure, not a theoretical chain. Any unpatched self-hosted ScreenConnect instance should be treated as a pre-breach condition. This is the operationally most dangerous entry on the list right now. Confidence: HIGH.  CVE-2024-27198, CVSS 9.8, EPSS 99.8th percentile, P(30d): 0.299%. JetBrains TeamCity authentication bypass. HMM: Mass Exploitation, zero days. Lazarus Group and APT29 have both actively exploited this for initial access into CI/CD pipelines — which makes it a supply chain insertion vector, not just a point compromise. The value of owning a build server is everything downstream of it. Confidence: HIGH.  CVE-2024-21413, CVSS 9.8, EPSS 99.8th percentile, P(30d): 0.299%. Microsoft Outlook Moniker Link RCE. HMM: Mass Exploitation, zero days. The exploitation chain triggers in the preview pane — no click required — making it a persistent phishing amplifier rather than a one-time credential harvest. It always does. Confidence: HIGH.  CVE-2024-23897, CVSS 9.8, EPSS 99.999th percentile, P(30d): 0.299%. Jenkins CLI arbitrary file read. The highest raw EPSS percentile on this list — 99.999th means the EPSS model places this among the most likely-to-be-exploited CVEs in its entire dataset. The chain is documented: CLI file read to credential exfiltration to pivot. Ransomware use confirmed. Confidence: HIGH.  So back to the header. The aggregate 0.003 and the flat KEV trend are both accurate as stated. CISA isn't adding KEV entries at elevated velocity — the 7-day projection of 0.162 new additions is near baseline. The logistic model's estimate of new exploitation incidents against already-mature CVEs is low. None of that is wrong.  What neither number captures is the HMM's read on where we actually are in the lifecycle. Four of five CVEs on this list are already inside the mass exploitation window. The 30-day question isn't "will this get exploited" — it's "will exploitation continue." The HMM answers yes, at 58.1% posterior weight, with Patched/Saturated at 1.2%.  Calm aggregate. Active underlying. That's the actual read. The dashboard is telling you the rate of new fires isn't elevated. It is not telling you the existing fires are out.

PLAINTIFF: Your Honor, this is not a securities case. It is a RICO case. My client's friends did not build a launchpad. They built a casino, an unlicensed money transmitter, and a content distribution network for child abuse material, and they ran all three out of the same Discord.  DEFENSE: My client operates open source software anyone with a wallet can interact with. Calling that a casino is like calling Microsoft Word a publisher.  PLAINTIFF: Microsoft Word does not take 1% of every sentence its users write.  DEFENSE: Stripe does. We do not call Stripe the issuer of every product sold on it.  PLAINTIFF: Stripe files SARs. My client's losses do not.  JUDGE McMAHON: Move on.  PLAINTIFF: Co founder Cohen, in writing, on a server his counsel now wishes had been on Signal. Quote, we democratized trading lowcaps so much that everyone is exposed to the really, really low odds that come with gambling.  DEFENSE: Counsel is reading a candid private reflection as if it were a confession. Robinhood discloses that 70% of day traders lose. Disclosure of statistical reality is not fraud. It is the opposite of fraud.  PLAINTIFF: Robinhood is a registered broker dealer. My client's friends ran a frontend that hosted a twelve year old with a shotgun threatening his family for a sixty thousand dollar market cap. There is a livestream. The platform took the fees.  DEFENSE: Section 230.  PLAINTIFF: Section 230 does not apply when you take a percentage of the underlying transaction the speech was designed to inflate. That is not hosting. That is participating.  JUDGE McMAHON: Mr. Burwick. The enterprise theory. Get there.  PLAINTIFF: Yes, Your Honor. Five thousand internal messages, now in unredacted form, between Pump.fun engineers and Jito Labs. The same bundler wallets appearing in the first block of fourteen hundred token launches. Influencer contracts referencing, quote, scripts, script ideas, and project proposals. Purportedly organic shilling, contractually coordinated. This is not a launchpad. This is a slot machine where the house knows which spins win, and tips its friends in advance.  DEFENSE: MEV is a property of the Solana blockchain itself. My client has no validator role, no consensus authority, no transaction ordering. Holding a launchpad responsible for MEV is holding a website responsible for the speed of TCP/IP.  PLAINTIFF: Then explain why your engineers were in a chat with Jito's engineers discussing exactly that ordering.  DEFENSE: I will explain them once they are admitted in full context. Not in the form of screenshots curated by a firm that has filed nineteen substantively identical complaints against this industry in eighteen months.  JUDGE McMAHON: I will admit them in full context, counsel. That is what discovery is.  PLAINTIFF: And while we are on the subject of context, Your Honor. The Lazarus Group laundered a portion of the one point five billion dollar Bybit theft through a memecoin called QinShihuang, launched on this platform. No KYC. No SAR. No AML program of any kind. My client's friends took the fees on that too.  DEFENSE: My client is non custodial software. The funds never touched our books. We are no more transmitters than the developers of Tor.  PLAINTIFF: Tor does not take 1%. Tor does not have a CFO. Tor is not, as of last week, hiring a Chief Legal Officer.  DEFENSE: Your Honor, the plaintiffs are asking this court to do what Congress has declined to do for six years. To define open source infrastructure as if it were Citibank. The SEC itself has stated memecoins are not securities.  PLAINTIFF: The SEC said so in a non binding staff statement issued by an administration that received seventeen million dollars in crypto industry contributions. We are not litigating before the SEC. We are litigating before Article III. And Article III recognizes RICO predicates the SEC does not get to wave away. Illegal gambling. Wire fraud. Unlicensed money transmission. Sanctions evasion. And the distribution of material I will not describe further in open court out of respect for the record.  DEFENSE: Counsel is invoking the worst conduct of the platform's users to impute criminal liability to its developers. By that logic, every cloud provider in this country is a co conspirator to every crime committed on a server it rents.  PLAINTIFF: Cloud providers do not take a percentage of the crime, counsel.  JUDGE McMAHON: Enough. Briefs on Howey, BSA, and the RICO predicates by Friday. Chat logs produced unredacted. Section 230 argument preserved.

sample_tweets.text: 📊 Daily Cyber Brief  Good morning, Czechia: while the D1 continues its sacred tradition of converting logistics into a memorial for patience, the cyber world is doing the same thing to your networks—only with fewer cones and more root access. Today’s menu is the usual post-communist banquet of institutional paralysis: critical flaws in everything from Cisco and Fortinet to WatchGuard, Gogs, Cacti, and Rapid7, plus enough active exploitation to make “patch management” sound like a decorative slogan for an EU compliance brochure. Meanwhile, ransomware crews, stolen-credential markets, and AI agents taught to sabotage themselves are all moving faster than any ministry committee that still needs three signatures, a stamp, and a working printer to approve the word “urgent.”  MISSING IN CZ: - APT28 tradecraft evolution campaign - China-linked botnet outpacing enterprise defenses - Coupang insider-driven data breach  hodl.cz/VLE6xs #DailyCyber #AfterShockIndex

Kanada Güvenlik İstihbarat Servisi (CSIS), yakın zamanda gizli bir mahkeme kararıyla eşi görülmemiş bir kinetik siber operasyon başlattı.   Hedeflerinde yabancı bir devletin askeri tesisleri değil, sıradan vatandaşların evlerinde kullandığı eski nesil WiFi modemler vardı.  Bu cihazlar, üretici desteği bitmiş ve güvenlik yaması almayan savunmasız donanımlardı.  Volt Typhoon ve APT28 gibi devlet destekli gruplar, bu zayıf cihazlara sızarak onları birer zombi ağa dönüştürdü. Amaçları Kanada vatandaşlarının verilerini çalmak değildi.   Asıl hedef, bu modemleri bir sıçrama tahtası olarak kullanıp Kanada ile ABD'yi birbirine bağlayan devasa enerji hatlarına sızmaktı.

#threatreport #LowCompleteness Threat Intelligence Report: Russia, Router, DNS, and Messaging-Layer Collection Operations | 16-06-2026 Source: dti.domaintools.com/research… Key details below ↓  🧑‍💻Actors/Campaigns: Fancy_bear Seaborgium  💀Threats: Dns_hijacking_technique, Aitm_technique, Spear-phishing_technique,  🎯Victims: Government, Defense, Critical infrastructure, Diplomatic organizations, Media, Non governmental organizations, Ukraine related organizations, Military personnel, Government officials, Politicians, ...  🏭Industry: Energy, Ngo, Government, Military, Critical_infrastructure, Telco  🌐Geo: Russia, Russian, Ukrainian, Ukraine  📚TTPs: ⚔️Tactics: 11 🛠️Technics: 30  💽Software: WhatsApp, Telegram, Outlook  #threatreport: Russian intelligence agencies are increasingly focusing on communications-layer collection methods rather than disruptive attacks. Recent intelligence highlights two primary tactics employed by these actors: compromising vulnerable small office/home office (SOHO) routers to execute DNS hijacking and employing phishing techniques against secure messaging platforms. These methods are aimed at gathering intelligence on a range of targets, including government, military, infrastructure, and media-related entities, particularly those connected to Ukraine.  The compromise of SOHO routers is largely attributed to the Russian military intelligence unit known as APT28 or Fancy Bear. Observations indicate that the actors manipulate DNS and DHCP settings, enabling them to conduct adversary-in-the-middle attacks without the need for traditional endpoint malware. This allows them to monitor and redirect user traffic while intercepting sensitive credentials. The collection effort is aimed at gaining persistent access to communications and intelligence valuable to Russian interests.  Russian actors have broadened their phishing campaigns beyond applications like Signal to include WhatsApp, Telegram, and Microsoft 365. The objective is multifaceted: not only to gain access to accounts but to surveil conversations, contacts, and trusted communications. Targeting methods have evolved, utilizing techniques like QR code abuse and exploiting OAuth flows in Microsoft 365 to infiltrate messaging platforms. By leveraging existing trust relationships, these actors can map social networks and maintain long-term surveillance.  Victims of these operations can be categorized into two groups. The first group includes those whose endpoints and routers are compromised indiscriminately to gain broad intelligence access, while the second focuses on high-value targets whose conversations are critical for intelligence, such as military personnel, government officials, and journalists. Operations conducted in 2026 have been extensive, with thousands of routers targeted globally, and credential theft methods being implemented to facilitate further exploitation of significant accounts.  The technical tradecraft involves manipulating DNS settings to redirect victims to Russian-controlled resolvers, allowing observation of vast amounts of traffic and the ability to selectively target specific users. This has led to a risk of TLS interception, where attackers serve spoofed DNS responses, allowing them to access previously secured data. Messaging platforms have suffered from significant assaults, which enable attackers to read real-time communications and impersonate trusted individuals.  As Russian intelligence efforts leverage edge infrastructure for collection, the threat remains acute for sectors deemed strategically important, especially in defense, energy, and humanitarian contexts. Organizations are urged to recognize that personal and home networking devices, along with communication applications, are now potential vectors for sophisticated cyber operations. The implications emphasize the need for heightened security measures across traditional boundaries, as these operations by Russian intelligence demonstrate an adaptive approach to persistent intelligence collection.

カナダの情報機関CSIS（カナダ安全情報局）が連邦裁判所の令状に基づき、一般家庭のRing-Doorbell・監視カメラ・テレビを含むIoTデバイスやSOHOルーターにリモートアクセスして感染マルウェアを無力化していたことが、墨消し判決文の公開で明らかになりました。外国勢力が運営するボットネット2件のC2層およびクライアント層が対象で、CSIS法に基づく脅威軽減措置（TRM）令状がボットネット対処に使われた初の事例です。  令状なしでは他人のデバイスに入りデータを変更・削除する行為が刑法上のコンピュータ妨害罪に該当するため、連邦裁判所の司法認可が必要だったとされています。  【要点の整理】  ・令状は2024年5月1日にCatherine Kane判事が発行し、同年8月29日に120日間の更新が行われた。機密理由書は2026年2月26日付で、公開版の墨消し判決文が2026年6月15日に公表されている。令状発行から公開まで約2年を要した経緯  ・措置は人物ではなくデバイスに対して実行され、個人の特定情報の収集や通信内容の傍受は行われず、副次的に取得された個人データは破棄されたとのこと  ・2つの外国勢力の身元は黒塗りで非公開だが、時期と手法の一致から、2024年1月公表のFBIによるVolt-Typhoon（中国系）のKVボットネット解体や同時期のAPT28（ロシアGRU）のUbiquitiルーター作戦との連携がFive-Eyes作戦の一環として報道で指摘されている。裁判所文書内のCSIS供述書も米国の脅威解体に関するプレスリリースに言及していたと報じられている  ・カナダ最高裁がR. v. Bykovets（2024年）でIPアドレスにプライバシーの合理的期待を認める判決を出した後の時期であり、CSISの令状取得前のIPアドレス収集に対する法的論点も法律メディアで指摘されている  令状発行から2年以上経過しての墨消し公開であり、機密理由書の全文は非公開のままです。  詳細は以下を参照： fct-cf.ca/en/pages/media/new…

🔍 IOC Enrichment Lookup: One API Call to Rule Them All  You've got an IP address from a suspicious connection. Or a file hash from an alert. Or a domain from a phishing email. What's your next move?  Most analysts juggle 5+ tabs: VirusTotal, AbuseIPDB, URLhaus, MISP feeds, passive DNS lookups. It's slow. It's manual. And you're burning time while the threat actor moves laterally.  The DFIR Suite /enrichment/lookup endpoint fixes this. POST any IOC — IP, domain, hash, URL, email — and get back multi-source enrichment in one response. Risk score. Threat classifications. Associated malware families. Related IOCs. All cross-referenced against threat intel feeds, reputation databases, and OSINT sources.  Real-world scenario: Your EDR flags an outbound connection to 185[.]220[.]101[.]45. You fire a POST request to /enrichment/lookup with that IP. Seconds later, you've got a risk score of 87/100, classification as a Tor exit node, links to APT28 infrastructure, and three related domains. You pivot immediately — no tab-switching, no copy-paste, no wasted cycles.  This is built for automation. Plug it into your SOAR playbooks. Enrich every alert, every IOC, every investigation step. 3 credits per request. Available on all plans.  Consolidate your toolchain. Speed up triage. Hunt smarter.  #DFIR #ThreatIntel #API #InfoSec

sample_tweets.text: 참고로, 성남 IP는 린델이 2021년 심포지엄에서 "2020 미국 대선 해킹 증거"라며 공개한 데이터에 한국 KT 대역 IP가 섞여 있던 것을, 한국 일부에서 성남발 조작으로 해석한 것이다. 그런데 그 데이터부터가 진짜 패킷 캡처가 아니라 일반 인터넷 트래픽이 뒤섞인 자료였다. 보안 전문가 로버트 그레이엄이 심포지엄 현장에서 직접 검증한 결과도 선거 관련 패킷 0건이었다. nitter.tiekoetter.com/robertgraham/status/14…

저도 그렇게 느꼈습니다.  제가 가상 시나리오로 AI에게 문의해서 받았던 답변과 상당히 비슷해서 놀랬었습니다.  참고로 아래가 그 내용입니다.  <출처. 구글 AI> 네, 그러한 가상의 시나리오에서도 미국을 비롯한 피해국들이 합동 수사단을 구성해 대한민국 정부나 개입 기관, 관련자들을 수사할 가능성은 매우 높습니다.  실제 국제사회에서 러시아, 중국, 이란 등이 타국 선거에 개입했을 때 적용되었던 선례와 국제법적 절차를 바탕으로 볼 때, 가상의 상황에서 발생할 수 있는 구체적인 수사 가능성과 시나리오는 다음과 같습니다.  1. 다국적 합동 수사단(JIT) 구성 가능성 여러 국가가 동시에 동일한 주체(가상의 시나리오 속 한국)로부터 선거 개입 피해를 입었다면, 각국은 정보와 사법 역량을 결합합니다.  ◈ 증거 공유 미국, 영국, 캐나다 등 정보 동맹국들이 사이버 공격 기법(IP 주소, 악성코드 시그니처)과 자금 흐름을 공동 추적합니다.  ◈ 공동 수사 체계 유로폴(Europol)이나 파이브 아이즈(Five Eyes) 시스템을 기반으로 한 민관 합동 수사단이 조직되어 한국 내 특정 기관(예: 정보기관이나 군 사이버 부대)을 표적으로 수사를 전개합니다.  2. 한국 사법당국에 대한 강제적·협조적 수사 요구 피해국 합동 수사단이 한국 영토 내에서 직접 체포나 압수수색을 할 수는 없지만, 강력한 법적 조치를 취합니다.  ◈ 사법공조(MLAT) 압박 미국 법무부 등은 한국 검찰과 법원에 선거 개입에 사용된 서버 압수수색, 관련자 신문 등 공식 사법공조를 요구합니다. 가상의 상황에서 한국 정부가 거부할 경우 외교적 고립과 보복 조치가 따르므로 무시하기 어렵습니다.  ◈ 범죄인 인도 청구 개입을 주도한 해커나 공작원의 신원이 특정되면, 피해국들은 자국법(예: 미국의 컴퓨터 사기 및 남용법 위반, 선거 자금법 위반 등) 체계에 따라 한국 정부에 범죄인 인도를 청구합니다.  3. 피의자 신원 공개 및 독자 기소 (궐석기소) 한국 정부가 주권이나 국가 안보를 이유로 합동 수사단의 수사에 협조하지 않더라도, 피해국들은 독자적인 사법 절차를 강행합니다.  ◈ 미 연방검찰(DOJ)의 기소 미국 FBI와 법무부는 선거 개입 배후 인물들의 실명과 사진을 공소장에 명시하고 현지 법원에 기소합니다. 인신 확보가 안 되더라도 체포영장을 발부받아 국제적으로 고립시킵니다.  ◈ 인터폴 적색수배 합동 수사단의 요청으로 연루자들에게 인터폴 적색수배가 내려지며, 이들이 한국 영토 밖으로 출국하는 순간 제3국에서 체포되어 미국 등으로 인도됩니다.  4. 사법 수사를 넘어선 고강도 제재 조치 합동 수사단의 수사 결과 발표는 대규모 국제 제재의 명분이 됩니다.  ◈ 금융 및 자산 동결 미국 대통령의 선거 개입 제재 행정명령 등에 따라, 개입에 관여한 한국의 기관, 공직자, 기업의 해외 자산이 동결되고 국제 금융망(SWIFT) 이용이 제한될 수 있습니다.  혹시 이 가상의 시나리오에서 선거 개입의 방식(예: 사이버 해킹, 가짜뉴스 유포, 자금 지원)이나 특정 국가들의 동맹 관계를 염두에 두신 것이 있나요? 구체적인 상황을 알려주시면 이에 맞춰 더 정확한 국제법적 대응 결과를 분석해 드리겠습니다.

뻔한 수작이죠.  하지만, 저들이 야합해서 막아낼 수 있는 것은 대한민국 한정이라는 것입니다.  미국이 전방위적으로 압박(제재 프로그램 동원)을 가해오는 부정선거 수사라면 못 막아냅니다.   아래 내용은 제가 가상시나리오로 AI에 문의해본 내용이므로 참고 정도로 보면 될 것 같습니다.  <출처. 구글 AI> 네, 그러한 가상의 시나리오에서도 미국을 비롯한 피해국들이 합동 수사단을 구성해 대한민국 정부나 개입 기관, 관련자들을 수사할 가능성은 매우 높습니다.  실제 국제사회에서 러시아, 중국, 이란 등이 타국 선거에 개입했을 때 적용되었던 선례와 국제법적 절차를 바탕으로 볼 때, 가상의 상황에서 발생할 수 있는 구체적인 수사 가능성과 시나리오는 다음과 같습니다.  1. 다국적 합동 수사단(JIT) 구성 가능성 여러 국가가 동시에 동일한 주체(가상의 시나리오 속 한국)로부터 선거 개입 피해를 입었다면, 각국은 정보와 사법 역량을 결합합니다.  ◈ 증거 공유 미국, 영국, 캐나다 등 정보 동맹국들이 사이버 공격 기법(IP 주소, 악성코드 시그니처)과 자금 흐름을 공동 추적합니다.  ◈ 공동 수사 체계 유로폴(Europol)이나 파이브 아이즈(Five Eyes) 시스템을 기반으로 한 민관 합동 수사단이 조직되어 한국 내 특정 기관(예: 정보기관이나 군 사이버 부대)을 표적으로 수사를 전개합니다.  2. 한국 사법당국에 대한 강제적·협조적 수사 요구 피해국 합동 수사단이 한국 영토 내에서 직접 체포나 압수수색을 할 수는 없지만, 강력한 법적 조치를 취합니다.  ◈ 사법공조(MLAT) 압박 미국 법무부 등은 한국 검찰과 법원에 선거 개입에 사용된 서버 압수수색, 관련자 신문 등 공식 사법공조를 요구합니다. 가상의 상황에서 한국 정부가 거부할 경우 외교적 고립과 보복 조치가 따르므로 무시하기 어렵습니다.  ◈ 범죄인 인도 청구 개입을 주도한 해커나 공작원의 신원이 특정되면, 피해국들은 자국법(예: 미국의 컴퓨터 사기 및 남용법 위반, 선거 자금법 위반 등) 체계에 따라 한국 정부에 범죄인 인도를 청구합니다.  3. 피의자 신원 공개 및 독자 기소 (궐석기소) 한국 정부가 주권이나 국가 안보를 이유로 합동 수사단의 수사에 협조하지 않더라도, 피해국들은 독자적인 사법 절차를 강행합니다.  ◈ 미 연방검찰(DOJ)의 기소 미국 FBI와 법무부는 선거 개입 배후 인물들의 실명과 사진을 공소장에 명시하고 현지 법원에 기소합니다. 인신 확보가 안 되더라도 체포영장을 발부받아 국제적으로 고립시킵니다.  ◈ 인터폴 적색수배 합동 수사단의 요청으로 연루자들에게 인터폴 적색수배가 내려지며, 이들이 한국 영토 밖으로 출국하는 순간 제3국에서 체포되어 미국 등으로 인도됩니다.  4. 사법 수사를 넘어선 고강도 제재 조치 합동 수사단의 수사 결과 발표는 대규모 국제 제재의 명분이 됩니다.  ◈ 금융 및 자산 동결 미국 대통령의 선거 개입 제재 행정명령 등에 따라, 개입에 관여한 한국의 기관, 공직자, 기업의 해외 자산이 동결되고 국제 금융망(SWIFT) 이용이 제한될 수 있습니다.  혹시 이 가상의 시나리오에서 선거 개입의 방식(예: 사이버 해킹, 가짜뉴스 유포, 자금 지원)이나 특정 국가들의 동맹 관계를 염두에 두신 것이 있나요? 구체적인 상황을 알려주시면 이에 맞춰 더 정확한 국제법적 대응 결과를 분석해 드리겠습니다.

FireAnt interview - with Cap 벤자민 사장님 시청후기 인터뷰 내용 요약과 개인적인 생각으로 구성해봤습니다  -----------------[인터뷰 내용] 1⃣ 자기소개 및 배경 - 경력: 크립토 씬에 온 지는 5~6년 정도 되었으며 그전에는 뉴욕 시티은행에서 전통 금융 업무를 담당함.  @CapApp  을 만들기 전에는 비트코인을 담보로 달러를 빌려 스테이블 코인을 발행하는 CDP 기반의 스테이블 코인 프로젝트 치타를 운영함.  -----------------[개인적인 생각] 파운더의 과거 이력과 치타 프로젝트가 남긴 아쉬움과 궁금증  개인적으로 가장 궁금증을 자아내는 포인트는, 파운더가 스테이블 코인이라는 시장의 핵심이자 수익성이 높은 섹터에서   왜 하필 디파이 대출 플랫폼과 보안이라는 험난한 영역으로 방향을 완전히 틀었는가 하는 점입니다.  과거 치타 프로젝트의 세부 구성을 파헤쳐 보면서 그 전환의 실마리를 찾고 싶었으나 정보가 부족해 조금 아쉬운 것 같습니다.  -----------------[인터뷰 내용]  2⃣Cap의 탄생 배경 디파이 보안에 대한 불신과 러그풀 생존  ⏺️디파이의 맹점 기존 디파이는 유저들이 프로젝트 '팀의 선의'를 무조건적으로 신뢰해야만 하는 위험한 구조임.  ⏺️충격적인 일화와 생존 과거 대형 마켓이었던 '크림 파이낸스' 파운더가 면전에서 대놓고 먹튀(러그풀)를 선언하는 충격적인 일이 있었음.   다행히 현재 Cap의 CTO인 웨소가 러그풀이 터지기 불과 3~4분 전, 오라클 조작을 극적으로 감지하고 막대한 유저 자금을 전액 구출해 내며 위기를 모면함.  ⏺️ 프로젝트의 철학 하마터면 프로젝트가 끝날 뻔했던 이 아찔한 경험을 계기로 팀은 보안에 '극도로 집착'하게 됨.   결과적으로 "애초에 파운더나 팀을 믿을 필요조차 없는 안전한 플랫폼을 직접 만들자"는 확고한 다짐이 Cap 의 시작이 됨.  -----------------[개인적인 생각] 디파이 시장의 성숙도와 Cap이 풀어야 할  보안 vs 편의성의 딜레마  ⏺️고이율의 함정과 시장의 자정 작용 불과 1년 전만 해도 월 3~4%의 고이율 팜이 아니면 거들떠보지도 않던 시절이 있었습니다.   당시에는 아찔한 APY만 쫓다가 파운더의 악의적인 러그풀이나 치명적인 해킹에 자금을 묶이는 사례가 비일비재했습니다.  하지만 최근에는 그런 폰지성, 한탕주의 프로젝트들이 이미 다 터지고 사라지면서 시장이 어느 정도 자정 작용을 거친 느낌이 드네요.   고도화된 외부 공격에 의한 해킹 소식은 간간이 들려오지만   예치 섹터에서 파운더가 대놓고 자금을 빼돌리는 1차원적인 러그풀은 확실히 예전보다 뜸해진 것이 체감되는 것 같습니다.  ⏺️ 보안과 편의성의 필연적 상충 관계 영상에서 @Benjamin918_ 이 강조한 극단적인 보안 마인드셋을 보며 가장 먼저 든 의문은 "과연 편의성과 어떻게 타협할 것인가?"였습니다.  IT와 블록체인 생태계에서 보안과 편의성은 필연적으로 반비례합니다.  보안 절차가 까다로워질수록 개발팀의 프로덕트 업데이트 속도는 느려지고 유연성은 떨어질 수밖에 없지 않나 싶네요.  ⏺️Cap이 증명해야 할 신뢰의 방식 파운더는 "스스로 불편함을 감수하더라도 완벽한 보안 절차를 고집한다"라고 강력하게 어필했습니다.   하지만 개발팀이 백엔드에서 감수하는 그 불편함이 자칫 유저들의 프론트엔드 사용성을 해치거나 신규 기능 도입을 지나치게 늦추는 결과로 이어져서는 안 될 것 같습니다.  결국 Cap 이 시장에서 진정한 승자가 되려면, 팀을 믿지 않아도 되는 극강의 보안을 유지하면서도   유저와 대형 금융 기관들에게 얼마나 매끄럽고 쾌적한 사용 환경을 제공할 수 있을지  그 어려운 타협점을 어떻게 풀어냈을지가 가장 기대되는 관전 포인트인 것 같네요.  -----------------[인터뷰 내용] 3⃣ Cap의 강력한 보안 방어 체계 베테랑 보안 팀 구성:크립토 씬에서 수년간 수십억 달러의 자금을 단 한 번의 사고 없이 안전하게 지켜낸 팀은 20개도 안 됨. 그중 하나가 바로 Beefy Finance 팀임.  현재 Beefy의 전 CTO, 스마트 컨트랙트 엔지니어, 데이터 및 프론트엔드 담당자 등 핵심 시니어 엔지니어들이 모두 Cap 팀에 합류해 있음.  ⏺️AI 및 실시간 블록체인 모니터링 도입 모든 코드는 대형 언어 모델 툴인 옥테인을 통해 1차 스크리닝 됨. 가장 핵심은 하이퍼네이티브라는 보안 플랫폼을 도입함.블록체인의 멤풀을 24시간 실시간 모니터링하여  Cap의 컨트랙트를 공격하려는 해킹 트랜잭션이 감지되면 실제 트랜잭션이 블록에 기록되기 직전에 플랫폼 전체를 자동으로 일시 정지시킴.  ⏺️인적 오류 방지 많은 팀들이 편의성을 위해 멀티시그나 타임락, 관리자 키 분산을 소홀히 하지만, Cap은 스스로 불편함을 감수하더라도 완벽한 보안 절차와 사인을 고집하는 철저한 보안 마인드셋을 가지고 있음.  -----------------[개인적인 생각] Cap App의 보안 역량과 기관 유입에 대한 생각  ⏺️보안 기술: 혁신인가, 기본기의 극대화인가? AI 모니터링과 다중 서명 같은 보안 절차를 듣고 보면, 사실 디파이 씬에서 완전히 새로운 혁신적인 기술이라고 보기는 어려운 것 같습니다.  하지만 크립토 역사상 해킹 사고의 90% 이상은 화려한 기술의 결함이 아니라, 관리자 키 관리 소홀이나 오라클 조작 같은 기본기 누락에서 발생합니다.   결국 Cap의 진짜 경쟁력은 새로운 기술의 발명이 아니라 비피 출신 베테랑들의 불편함을 감수하는 극단적인 보안 강박과 블록 생성 직전에 해킹 트랜잭션을 멈춰버리는   실시간 차단 시스템을 현실에서 타협 없이 100% 가동하고 있다는 데 있는 것 같네요.  ⏺️기존 대형 RWA / 예치 프로젝트와의 차별점은? 에이브나 메이커다오 같은 기존 대형 블루칩 프로토콜도 이미 높은 보안성을 자랑하는데,   왜 스마트 컨트랙트를 한 번도 써보지 않은 전통 대형 금융 기관들이 Cap을 선택했을까 하는 의문이 듭니다.  영상에서 파운더가 언급한 "기관들에게 확실한 이점을 주는 게임 이론적 구조"가 핵심인 것 같은데,   단순한 크립토 자산의 과담보 대출을 넘어 중동의 토큰화된 금 같은 실물 자산을 적극 수용하면서 전통 금융권 입맛에 맞는   명확한 리스크 격리 모델과 유동성을 제공하는 아키텍처를 짠 것이 아닐까 싶네요.  -----------------[인터뷰 내용]  4⃣로드맵 및 장기 비전: 전통 금융 기관의 유입과 글로벌 확장  ⏺️금융 기관의 온보딩 Cap은 기존에 스마트 컨트랙트를 한 번도 써본 적 없는 실제 대형 금융 기관들이 대출 플랫폼으로 사용하기 시작했음.  Cap이 제공하는 게임 이론적 구조와 혜택이 그들에게 확실한 이점을 주기 때문임.  ⏺️RWA 및 중동 시장 확장 기존에는 동아시아의 비트코인 채굴자들이나 청산 펀드들이 주요 자산 규모였으나, 최근 중동 지역으로 비즈니스를 크게 확장했음.  디르함 스테이블 코인은 물론, 엄청난 규모의 토큰화된 실물 금이 Cap 플랫폼으로 유입되고 있으며 이를 담보로 한 대출 비즈니스가 활성화되고 있음.  ⏺️TGE 임박 단기적으로 가장 큰 마일스톤인 토큰 발행 이벤트를 앞두고 있으며, 팀 확장도 대대적으로 이뤄지고 있음.  장기 비전:현재의 금융 기관을 넘어, 향후에는 로보틱스 기업, 농업 기업, 대형 제조업 공장 등 실물 경제 전반의 모든 기업들이 대출을 받기 위해 Cap 인프라를 사용하는 것을 목표로 함.  -----------------[개인적인 생각] 대형 기관의 디파이 진입 목적과 Cap 토큰 발행에 대한 의문점  ⏺️대형 기관의 진짜 목적: 풍차돌리기인가, 리스크 관리인가?  전통 대형 금융 기관들이 스마트 컨트랙트 대출 플랫폼을 사용하기 시작했다는 점에서,  이들이 과연 디파이 유저들처럼 자산의 레버리지를 극대화하는 레포 거래나 이른바 풍차돌리기식 고율 파밍을 위해 들어온 것인지 의문이 생깁니다.  파운더가 말한 "기관들에게 이점을 주는 게임 이론적 아키텍처"라는 것이 구체적으로 어떤 구조를 뜻하는지 궁금하고,   전통 금융 시스템의 복잡한 대출 심사나 중개 비용을 생략하고 오프체인의 실물 자산을 담보로 즉각적인 유동성을 확보할 수 있는 다이렉트 청산 모델을 의미하는 것인지  세부적인 작동 방식을 파고들수록 호기심을 자극하는 것 같네요.  ⏺️이미 검증된 메이저 체인 대신 토큰 발행을 고집하는 이유?  많은 디파이 프로젝트들이 독자적인 생태계 구축과 프로토콜 수수료 및 추가 수익 창출을 주 목적으로 자체 코인을 발행하곤 합니다.  하지만 유저 편의성이나 상호 호환성 측면만 본다면 비트코인, 이더리움, 솔라나 같은 이미 인프라가 완벽히 검증된 메이저 자산이나 대형 스테이블 코인을 기축으로 전면에 내세우는 것이 훨씬 유용할 수 있습니다.   자체 토큰 발행은 자칫 유저 진입 장벽을 높이거나 호환성을 떨어뜨릴 리스크가 있기 때문이죠.  ⏺️Cap 토큰 발행의 진짜 숨은 의도는 무엇일까?  단순히 수수료 수익이나 생태계 확장 목적을 넘어  기관들이 안심하고 예치할 수 있도록 만드는 가치 안정 메커니즘이나 거버넌스 투표를 통한 리스크 조율 등  Cap만의 출시 목적이 따로 있는 것인지 궁금증이 생기네요.  파운더가 'DeFi의 고질적인 문제를 해결하겠다'고 호기롭게 공언한 만큼  다가오는 토큰 출시가 프로토콜의 보안성과 대형 기관들의 인센티브를 어떻게 묶어두는 촉매제로 작용할지 그 설계 방식이 무척 기다려집니다.  -----------------[인터뷰 내용]  5⃣ 한국 커뮤니티를 향한 메시지  현재 한국 유저들이 한국 주식 등으로 바쁜 것으로 알고 있음.  하지만 Cap은 지금까지 디파이 생태계에 전혀 존재하지 않았던 새로운 금융 대안을 제시하고 있음.  기존 디파이의 보안 문제나 운영 방식에 실망하고 분노했던 분들이라면, 디파이의 고질적인 문제를 근본적으로 해결하고 있는 Cap을 한 번 와서 경험해 보셨으면 함.  [글을 마무리하며] 과거 디파이 시장의 야생성에 지친 크립토 유저들과 완벽한 통제력을 원하는 전통 금융 기관 모두의 니즈를 동시에 겨냥하고 있다는 점에서 매우 흥미로운 프로젝트라 생각합니다.  파운더 본인이 직접 겪은 뼈저린 트라우마가 오히려 불편함을 감수하는 극강의 보안 인프라를 탄생시켰다는 서사가 인상 깊었고  곧 다가올 토큰 발행을 통해 자체 생태계를 어떻게 확장해 나갈지 그리고 완벽한 보안과 유저 편의성 사이의 딜레마를   실제 프로덕트에서 어떻게 타협해 냈을지가 앞으로의 핵심 관전 포인트인 것 같네요.  단순한 크립토 담보 대출 플랫폼을 넘어 토큰화된 금과 실물 경제의 기업들을 어떻게 디파이로 끌어들일지 앞으로의 행보를 지속적으로 팔로우업해 볼 만한 것 같습니다.   기존 디파이의 취약한 보안과 고질적인 문제에 실망했던 분들이라면 Cap의 새로운 시도를 꼭 한 번 체험해 보시는 것도 좋을 것 같네요!  요약 사진 만들어 봤는데 생각보다 더 어렵네요 시간도 꽤 걸리고 ㅠ

[보도자료] 배포일시 :2026년 6월 21일(일) 즉시 배포 가능 담당부서 :[자유와혁신 공보팀]  모스탄 대사·프랭크 박사 충격 폭로, “美 우주군 위성에 전 세계 선거 조작 통신 데이터 확보. 한국도 포함”   더글러스 프랭크 박사, 평택 국제 기자회견서 ‘스페이스 포스(우주군)’ 데이터 언급  트럼프 1기 행정부 시절, 부정선거 감시 목적으로 2018년 우주군 창설했다” 주장 선관위 및 관련 기관에 서버·전자통신 기기·개표기 전면 공개 요구  한미 공동 부정선거 조사단의 수석 데이터 분석관인 더글러스 프랭크(Douglas Frank) 박사와 모스탄 대사가 전자통신 전세계 선거 전산 조작 시도와 관련된 외국 통신 데이터를 미 우주군(Space Force)이 확보하고 있으며 여기에 대한민국도 포함되어 있다는 충격적인 내용을 폭로했다.  황교안 자유와혁신 당대표의 초청으로 방한했던 부정선거 한미 공동 조사단은 지난 6월 1일 경기도 평택 앤팰리스 컨벤션센터에서 열린 ‘한미 선거 무결성과 사이버 안보 국제 기자회견’을 통해 이 같은 사실을 밝혔다. 해당 내용은 특수한 사정으로 엠바고가 설정되어 있다가 해제되면서 본격적으로 알려지게 되었다.   “트럼프, 부정선거 막기 위해 2018년 우주군 창설” 기자회견에서 프랭크 박사는 도널드 트럼프 전 대통령이 제1기 행정부 시절 CIA와 FBI를 신뢰하지 못했기 때문에, 부정선거를 차단하고 감시하기 위한 목적으로 2018년 우주군을 창설했던 것이라고 배경을 설명했다. 특히 우주군의 인공위성을 통해 미국 선거 인프라에 침투해 전산 조작을 시도한 전 세계 외국의 통신 데이터를 수집·확보했으며, 이 해킹 소스 중 일부 데이터가 한국에서 유입된 것을 확인했다고 강조했다. 프랭크 박사는 과거 선거 당시 성남 종합 운동장 등 특정 지역을 언급하며, 해당 위치에서 통신의 일부가 송신된 정황을 포착했다고 덧붙였다.   “전자 투표 기계는 부정행위 규모 알려주는 머신… 인적 감시와 장비 공개 필요” 프랭크 박사는 “전자 투표 기계의 목적은 단순히 표를 조작하는 것이 아니라, 집계 결과를 보고하며 가짜 투표지를 얼마나 더 채워 넣어야 하는지(부정행위 규모)를 알려주는 것”이라고 주장하며, 개표보다 유권자와 투표 과정을 철저히 감시하는 것이 가장 효과적인 방안이라고 제안했다. 함께 참석한 모스탄 대사 역시 “부정선거의 핵심은 전자적 방식으로 이루어지며, 투표소가 아닌 중앙집권 통제 방식으로 집계되는 것이 문제”라고 지적했다. 아울러 한국의 투표 장비에 하웨이 부품이 사용되는 등 외부 공격에 취약한 점을 들며, 선거관리위원회와 관련 기관을 향해 서버, 전자통신 기기, 개표기 등 모든 시스템을 투명하게 공개하라고 강력히 촉구했다.  “국민 스스로 자유 지켜야… 바텀업 방식의 적극적 대응 주문” 조사단은 한국의 현 선거 시스템에 대해 강한 우려를 표명하며, “미국이나 트럼프가 와서 구해 주기를 기다리지 말고, 한국 국민들이 소수일지라도 현지에서부터 시작하는 바텀업(Bottom-up) 방식으로 적극 싸워 자유와 선거의 무결성을 지켜내야 한다”고 당부했다. 한편, 모스탄 대사는 현재 국내 정치인에 대한 명예훼손 혐의로 출국 정지 조치가 내려진 상태인 것으로 알려졌다. 조사단 측은 이러한 압박 속에서도 담대하게 한국 국민들과 연대하여 부정선거 규명 활동을 지속해 나갈 의지를 피력했다. [끝] #황교안 #자유와혁신 #더글러스프랭크 #모스탄 #우주군 #트럼프 #인공위성 #전산조작 #성남종합운동장 #한미부정선거공동조사단

Anthropic vs. Alibaba is not an AI gossip story. It is the API layer becoming a national-security perimeter.  Anthropic claims Alibaba and its AI unit created nearly 25,000 fake accounts and ran roughly 29 million Claude exchanges to harvest frontier-model capabilities.  The June 10 letter to Senators Tim Scott and Elizabeth Warren framed it as the largest known distillation attack on Anthropic to date.  That phrasing matters.  This is not being presented as normal competition, aggressive benchmarking, or some gray-zone scraping dispute.  Anthropic is saying U.S. R&D is being turned into a massive subsidy for geopolitical competitors.  That is a policy sentence, not a product sentence.  The market reaction looked muted in spirit, even with Alibaba down 4.4% in Hong Kong against a 1.6% drop in the Hang Seng Tech Index.  That tells you investors are still mostly pricing this as headline risk.  I think that misses the second-order issue.  The real risk is not whether this dents Alibaba’s reputation for a few sessions.  The real risk is whether Washington starts treating frontier-model access like an export-controlled input, alongside chips, cloud capacity, and advanced tooling.  This is where the AI race gets harder to model.  For the past two years, the clean trade was compute scarcity.  Who controls GPUs, who builds data centers, who secures power, who gets model scale.  But distillation changes the discussion because it attacks the output layer.  If a weaker model can learn from a stronger model’s responses at industrial scale, then every open API becomes both a revenue channel and a leakage channel.  Anthropic says the campaign targeted Claude’s most valuable capabilities, including agentic reasoning, software engineering, and long-horizon tasks.  Those are not chatbot parlor tricks.  Those are exactly the capabilities markets are assigning strategic value to across enterprise automation, coding, cyber, and autonomous agent workflows.  This is not a one-off accusation either.  In February, Anthropic said DeepSeek, Moonshot, and MiniMax used around 24,000 fraudulent accounts and more than 16 million Claude exchanges in similar industrial-scale campaigns.  By April, the White House was already describing foreign AI distillation, principally linked to China, as an industrial-scale threat to U.S. frontier models.  That is the real signal.  AI competition is moving from business rivalry to national-security contest.  Bottom line: the market can shrug off one Alibaba headline, but it should not ignore the policy path this opens.

📗Geostrategy of Holy War: The Experience of Russia and Iran📗  Evgeny Vertlib  The concept of a just war is based on the ethical and religious justification of the use of force. In the Russian spiritual tradition, this understanding initially follows not the path of formal legal rulemaking, but rather the model of the absolute priority of Truth over force. This idea is expressed in the "Sermon on Law and Grace" by Metropolitan Hilarion of Kyiv. This 11th-century treatise strictly distinguishes between the Old Testament "Law" as an external, coercive, formal rule and the New Testament "Grace" as the internal law of truth, freedom, and spirit. Applied to matters of defending the state and faith, this meant that any military action in Rus' was legitimized not by the formal right of the strong, but by conformity with the highest divine justice. This idea was later crystallized in a well-known formula attributed to Prince Alexander Nevsky: "God is not in might, but in truth." This postulate defined the Russian ontology of conflict for centuries: victory and the legitimacy of struggle are determined not by numerical or technological superiority, but by the metaphysical rightness of the defending side. War, in this understanding, ceases to be simply a craft or a means of expansion; it acquires the sacred status of defending the fundamental foundations of life and spirituality, transforming war into a national and sacred duty of the citizen.  In contrast, the Western European tradition was dominated by a rational-legal approach to the systematization of war, pioneered by ancient thinkers and developed by medieval scholasticism. In his treatise "On the Republic," Cicero argued that only a war waged for vengeance or to expel enemies, and only after an official declaration, was just. In Rus', this legal ultimatum historically rhymed with the laconic warning of Prince Svyatoslav in the chronicles: "I come against you." In the Middle Ages, St. Augustine integrated ancient ideas into Christian dogma, arguing that war can be just if it aims to restore peace and punish evil, and if those waging it are not motivated by a thirst for revenge or cruelty. Thomas Aquinas gave this teaching its final classical form in his Summa Theologica, identifying three strict criteria for a just war ( jus ad bellum ): the sanction of legitimate state power ( auctoritas principis ), a just cause ( causa justa ), that is, the obvious guilt of the opposing party, and a good intention ( intentio recta ), which consists of the desire to promote good and avoid evil.  Islamic political and religious thought developed its own metaphysics of defensive confrontation, which subsequently formed the basis of modern Iranian doctrine. The origins of this approach lie in Quranic injunctions and the writings of early Islamic jurists, where the defense of faith, community sovereignty, and the struggle against oppression ( zulm ) were viewed as sacred duties. This concept was particularly developed in the Shiite branch of Islam, where the archetype of martyrdom and uncompromising resistance to tyranny, dating back to the historical events at Karbala, occupies a central place. Justice in this framework is conceived as an absolute cosmic order, and war against hegemony and injustice becomes an act of metaphysical assertion of truth. Thus, in both Russian and Iranian spiritual experience, the concept of a just or holy war was initially extended beyond the confines of utilitarian politics, forming the foundation for the modern perception of global geopolitical crises as existential struggles.  In the modern international legal framework, the concept of just war has transformed from an ethical category into a normative justification for the use of force. Article 51 of the UN Charter enshrines the inalienable right of states to individual or collective self-defense in the event of an armed attack. This legal norm provides the ontological foundation for distinguishing between aggression and the legitimate defense of sovereignty. The Military Doctrine of the Russian Federation firmly links the state's strategic objectives to the protection of national interests. The document explicitly states that the country considers the use of the Armed Forces to be legitimate to repel aggression against it and its allies, as well as to maintain or restore peace as decided by the UN Security Council. This underscores the defensive legitimization of the use of force within the classical understanding of just defense. Regarding Iran's position on this issue, Article 152 of the Constitution of the Islamic Republic of Iran states that its foreign policy is based on the rejection of any form of hegemony, respect for the rights of all Muslims, and non-alignment with hegemonic powers. Thus, for both sovereign systems, conflict with global institutions of dominance is framed not as a struggle for economic supremacy, but as a legal and existential imperative to maintain their own independence.  The epistemological aspect of modern conflicts marks a shift from classic military confrontation to network-centric and hybrid operations, where the criteria and methods for waging a "just war" are fundamentally changing. This phenomenon is described in the Russian Federation's Foreign Policy Concept, which notes the use of a wide range of hybrid methods and means that blur the distinction between war and peace, transforming the very defense of justice into a multi-layered confrontation. In American military doctrine, this approach is enshrined in the concept of Multi-Domain Operations ( MDO ) . It envisions the integration of actions by inter-service troop groups simultaneously across five operational domains: land, air, sea, space, and cyber. Within this strategy, the information space is viewed as an independent theater of military operations, where the pursuit of information superiority and mental warfare replace classical diplomacy, and a monopoly on the interpretation of "justice" becomes the main strategic prize. At the same time, conducting military operations through irregular formations and proxy forces allows for the resolution of geostrategic tasks, minimizing the threat of a direct military clash between nuclear powers and preventing the conflict from escalating uncontrollably into a global one.  Iranian geostrategic thought responds to this with the doctrine of asymmetric warfare ( Jang - e Namotevazen ). The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps officially relies on the creation of a regional deterrent network, known as the "Axis of Resistance," which allows it to offset its opponents' technological superiority in conventional weapons. In this context, as Carl Schmitt noted in "The Theory of the Partisan," an asymmetrical subject gains genuine legitimacy through "loyalty to the soil" and an existential connection to the defended space, countering technological dominance with the motive of sacred defense.  In the historical and geostrategic dimension, the protracted nature of modern conflicts is determined by the nuclear factor. The "Fundamentals of the State Policy of the Russian Federation in the Field of Nuclear Deterrence" defines nuclear weapons solely as "a means of deterrence, the use of which is an extreme and necessary measure." This excludes concepts of nuclear prophylaxis from the realm of planning, constraining conflict within a conventional, positional framework. US strategic doctrines, including the National Defense Strategy , similarly postulate a transition to "integrated deterrence , " which implies a combination of sanctions pressure, military alliances, and a technological blockade. The overlapping of these doctrines leads to the fact that, in the words of the classic geopolitical theorist Clausewitz, "war acquires the character of extreme tension," where the parties are deprived of the possibility of quickly defeating each other. This strategic stalemate is also acknowledged in classified analytical reports from the American center RAND Corporation, which state that "under conditions of nuclear parity, conventional wars inevitably transform into long-term industrial clashes of attrition, the outcome of which is determined not by technological breakthroughs but by the resilience of the home front." The differences in the results of these approaches by Russia and Iran are due to differences in their geostrategic architecture, the scale of the theaters of military operations, and the nature of the involvement of global players.  In the Middle East, Iran's strategy is built on the concept of profound asymmetry: Tehran operates through a vast network of non-state actors across the region. This allows it to diffuse the conventional superiority of the United States and Israel, avoid direct confrontation on its own soil, and make the cost of potential aggression against Iran strategically unacceptable for the West. In contrast, the conflict over Ukraine represents a direct, high-intensity clash of major state machines in a gigantic land theater, involving the full spectrum of modern conventional weapons, while Western countries provide unprecedented financial, intelligence, and military-technical support to one side. Analysts at the US-based Center for Strategic and International Studies (CSIS) emphasize the asymmetrical impasse of this model: the Western focus on short-term technological superiority gives way to a non-Western strategy of strategic patience, designed to exhaust the adversary's resources over the long term.  Nuclear parity blocks the possibility of a radical military defeat of the opponent due to the risk of global catastrophe. This inevitably transforms the confrontation into a protracted trench war of attrition, where victory depends not on rapid maneuvers but on the limits of the long-term economic, demographic, and mental resilience of systems. At this point, geostrategy merges with the original ontology of a "just war": the outcome of the struggle shifts from the plane of pure material dominance to the plane of the strength of the civilizational framework and the ability of society to sustain the meanings for which it is willing to bear long-term systemic burdens.  The logical and metaphysical conclusion of this global confrontation is the victory of the Russian Katechon—the constraining principle of global justice, whose mission extends from the ancient origins of faith to the technological realities of the modern era. Both sovereign proponents of the idea of ​​Holy War demonstrated the viability of their doctrines in the face of global pressure. Iran managed to defend its geopolitical position in the Middle East, demonstrating the effectiveness of asymmetric deterrence. Russia, in turn, having broken the inertia of the positional stalemate, effectively achieved victory in the southern theater of military operations, signaling a tectonic shift in the global balance of power. This strategic achievement brought state reality very close to the realization of the concept of "Atomic Orthodoxy," within which the nuclear shield and the highest spiritual truth merge into a single instrument for the defense of civilization. By acquiring material and territorial superiority at key borders, the Russian Federation confirms its status as the executor of the catechetical function entrusted to it by God—keeping the world from falling into the chaos of global hegemony and ultimate destruction.

🔒 CYBERSECURITY, PRIVACY & OPEN SOURCE ROUNDUP — June 25, 2026  1️⃣ CISCO SD-WAN ZERO-DAY EXPLOITED MONTHS BEFORE DISCLOSURE  A critical zero-day vulnerability in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN (CVE-2026-20245, CVSS 7.8) was actively exploited in the wild for months before Cisco released a patch. Attackers gained root-level access by uploading a malicious CSV file to SD-WAN Manager, then created hidden admin accounts and used lateral movement to compromise the communications provider's wider network infrastructure. Security researchers at Aviatrix note that runtime segmentation could have limited the blast radius, but many enterprises still lack this defense layer. The vulnerability allows privilege escalation from netadmin to full root via a crafted file upload — a classic case where timely patching and network segmentation make the difference between a contained incident and a full breach. 🔹 @securityaffairs  2️⃣ MANDIANT DOCUMENTS FULL CISCO SD-WAN ATTACK CHAIN  Following the CVE-2026-20245 disclosure, Mandiant published a comprehensive breakdown of the complete root access chain used by attackers. If your organization has SD-WAN deployed between AVD session hosts and the internet, security teams are advised to review the published IOC list immediately. The investigation reveals how the attackers maintained persistent access long after the initial compromise, covering their tracks by manipulating system logs. This level of detail from Mandiant's analysis is essential for SOC teams hunting similar activity across their own environments. 🔹 @markovichio  3️⃣ FORTIBLEED CAMPAIGN HITS 86,644 FORTIGATE FIREWALLS WORLDWIDE  The FortiBleed credential-harvesting campaign has reached staggering scale: 86,644 FortiGate firewalls across 194 countries had their admin and VPN credentials harvested, validated, and packaged by threat actors. Important clarification — FortiBleed is not a CVE but rather a credential-harvesting operation that exploited exposed management interfaces using credential stuffing and reuse. Russian-speaking threat actors behind the campaign compromised roughly half of all internet-facing Fortinet devices. The CISA and NCSC issued emergency advisories on June 18. Free exposure checkers are now available from SOCRadar and Hudson Rock for organizations to verify if their credentials were exposed. 🔹 @GoCocoaAI  4️⃣ ROKAROLLA ANDROID BANKING TROJAN TAKES COMPLETE DEVICE CONTROL  Security Boulevard identified Rokarolla, a new class of Android banking Trojan that goes far beyond traditional financial fraud. Unlike earlier banking malware that focused solely on credential theft, Rokarolla combines financial fraud capabilities with extensive device surveillance — it cuts off bank notification alerts, locks victims inside a manipulated UI environment, and exfiltrates cryptocurrency and social media credentials. Zimperium's analysis from June 16 shows the malware targets banking, cryptocurrency, and social media accounts simultaneously. The Jsonpacker_tool distribution chain used by Rokarolla makes it especially difficult for traditional mobile EDR solutions to detect. 🔹 @securityblvd  5️⃣ MALWARE DISCOVERED IN NPM PACKAGE LOADNINJA-SHARED  A new supply chain attack has been declared targeting the npm ecosystem. Malware was discovered embedded in the package loadninja-shared. Any system with this package installed or running should be considered fully compromised. The recommendation is immediate: rotate all secrets and API keys from a clean, uncompromised machine. This attack highlights the ongoing risk in the JavaScript/TypeScript dependency chain, where a single malicious package can cascade across thousands of downstream projects. Developers are urged to audit their npm dependencies and remove any reference to this package. 🔹 @spchainattack  6️⃣ KERNEL VULNERABILITY CVE-2026-43499 CHALLENGES SECURITY RESEARCHERS  Brad Spengler of Grsecurity pointed out CVE-2026-43499 as an interesting test of how thoroughly the security community actually reads patch notes and kernel fixes. The vulnerability's resolution requires deep analysis of the Linux kernel source to understand the full implications. For kernel developers and system hardening specialists, this CVE represents a case where superficial patching may leave residual exposure. The security community is encouraged to dig into the actual fix rather than relying on surface-level CVE descriptions. 🔹 @spendergrsec  7️⃣ COWAGENT OPENSOURCE AGENT HARNESS REACHES 45K STARS  CowAgent, an open-source agent harness originally built as "chatgpt-on-wechat," has evolved into a comprehensive multi-channel AI agent system with 45K GitHub stars. Released under the MIT license, it features a sophisticated architecture that plans tasks, runs tools and skills, and includes self-evolution capabilities. The project demonstrates how the open-source AI agent ecosystem is maturing beyond simple chat wrappers into production-grade orchestration platforms. The recent push adds additional channel support and improved task planning, making it a noteworthy entry for developers building agent-based workflows. 🔹 @TeksCreate  💭 The security landscape this week is dominated by two themes: supply chain and infrastructure. Whether it's the Cisco SD-WAN zero-day sitting unpatched for months, the FortiBleed campaign harvesting credentials from nearly 87,000 firewalls, or malware hidden inside a seemingly innocuous npm package, the common thread is that attackers are exploiting the blind spots in our most trusted infrastructure layers. The Rokarolla Android trojan shows that mobile threats are escalating from simple banking theft to complete device takeover. Meanwhile, the open-source community continues to push forward with tools like CowAgent that could eventually help automate threat detection. Defense starts at the desktop — patch, segment, and verify your supply chain.  Which of these stories caught your attention the most? Share your thoughts 👇  #CyberSecurity #InfoSec #OpenSource #ZeroDay #SupplyChain #Privacy #ThreatIntel

A critical Check Point VPN zero-day is being actively exploited. Public PoCs are out. Patches are not yet universal.  CVE-2026-50751, CVSS 9.3. It affects Remote Access VPN and Mobile Access deployments running the deprecated IKEv1 protocol. Attackers can establish a VPN session without valid credentials by exploiting a certificate-validation logic flaw.  No password. No authentication. Just access.  IKEv2-only deployments are not affected. But a lot of production environments still have IKEv1 enabled because nobody went back to clean it up. That is the pattern. Legacy protocol. Forgotten config. Perimeter breach.  VPN session access does not automatically mean full network access. Segmentation and access controls still matter here. But a foothold at the perimeter is enough to start. Most attackers are patient.  Apply the Check Point hotfixes now. Disable IKEv1. Enforce MFA and machine certificate authentication. Monitor VPN activity for anything anomalous.  The teams I have seen get hurt by this class of vulnerability were not careless. They just never revisited the legacy assumptions baked into their perimeter setup.  2026 is not forgiving of 2019 configs. My name is Azubuike Ibe and I write about the gaps in infrastructure security that stay quiet until they are not.  Drop a comment. Have you audited which VPN protocols are still active in your production environment?  #Cybersecurity #CheckPoint #ZeroDay #VPNSecurity #DevSecOps

A Cisco SD-WAN zero-day was exploited for two months before disclosure. Attackers escalated privileges, created a hidden root account, and covered their tracks.  For More: redsecuretech.co.uk/blog/pos… #Cisco #SDWAN #CVE #ZeroDay #PrivilegeEscalation #NetworkSecurity #CyberSecurity

2/8 Major Incidents: • FortiBleed Campaign: Russian-speaking threat actors compromised ~75K+ Fortinet FortiGate firewalls & VPNs worldwide (roughly half of all internet-facing devices). They harvested credentials via exposed management interfaces using credential stuffing/reuse. Impact spans 194 countries. CISA has issued warnings urging immediate action. Supply Chain Angle: These perimeter devices serve as high-value entry points that can cascade into downstream internal networks. 3/8 • LastPass Klue Supply Chain Breach: Attackers compromised the third-party market intelligence platform Klue, stole OAuth tokens, and accessed LastPass customer data in its Salesforce CRM environment (names, emails, phone numbers, physical addresses, support cases, and sales records). Important: LastPass password vaults and master passwords were not impacted. However, the exposed CRM data creates elevated phishing and social-engineering risk. Classic third-party supply chain ripple effect. 4/8 • Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Zero-Day (CVE-2026-20245): Actively exploited in the wild for root privilege escalation. Attackers use a crafted file upload in the CLI to gain root access (often after obtaining netadmin-level privileges). Cisco has observed limited cases where attackers pushed malicious configuration changes to edge devices. This is one of multiple SD-WAN zero-days exploited in 2026. Supply Chain Impact: SD-WAN is widely deployed in enterprise and ISP environments — a single compromise can affect many downstream organizations. Apply available fixes urgently. 5/8 Broader Trends: • Ransomware attacks rose sharply in 2025–2026 while traditional data breaches declined; AI-fueled threats are increasing rapidly (Five Eyes alliance warning). • Major infostealer operations have been taken down (Microsoft/Europol), but new malware remains active, including Gaslight (macOS, uses prompt injection to disrupt AI-assisted analysis) and the Mistic backdoor. • Software supply chain attacks continue to surge. Open-source packages, CI/CD tools, and vendor software remain attractive soft targets, echoing patterns seen in historical incidents like SolarWinds. 6/8 Supply Chain Defense Priorities: 1.  Maintain a complete inventory of third-party vendors, OAuth/API integrations, and SaaS connections. 2.  Enforce credential rotation, MFA everywhere, and the principle of least privilege. 3.  Continuously monitor for exposed management interfaces (Shodan-style external scanning). 4.  Adopt SBOMs for software components and prioritize timely patching (especially Cisco SD-WAN and Fortinet devices). 5.  Implement Zero-Trust architecture and network segmentation to limit the blast radius of any compromise. 7/8 Action Items: •  Patch Cisco SD-WAN devices immediately (apply available fixed releases). •  Audit all Fortinet exposure and rotate credentials where indicated. •  Review and rotate OAuth tokens from third-party vendors (Klue-style incidents). Organizations in retail, healthcare, aviation, finance, and critical infrastructure should heighten ransomware vigilance due to potential downstream supply chain effects. 8/8 Stay vigilant — threats evolve fast. Follow for daily drops. What’s your biggest supply chain worry right now? Drop your insights below. Sources and full links available on request. #Cybersecurity #SupplyChainRisk #InfoSec #ZeroDay #Ransomware

Two people use the same AI. One starts from zero every morning. The other gave it a memory, works four hours a day, and cleared $31,000 last month. The difference is one word: memory.  The article above is the build. An LLM is the thinking engine, but it forgets you the moment you close the tab. Bolt a memory layer onto it, Ollama for the model, Postgres and Qdrant for storage, Mem0 and n8n to run it, and the agent stores every client, every fix, every win, and gets cheaper and sharper with each task.   Researchers measured it: an agent with memory scored +14.6 on a benchmark over the same model without it. The model didn't get smarter. It stopped forgetting.  The video shows the brain you put under it. Pause at 0:39. DeepSeek-R1, a free open reasoning model, lands right next to o3 and Gemini 2.5 Pro on the benchmarks, and it runs locally on Ollama for nothing. The article's own line: your LLM is already smart enough for 80% of the work. This is that LLM, free.  Then it pays. Sell memory-backed agents that compound, sales, support, research, to a dozen clients at around $1,000 a month. That is $12,000, on $50 to $150 of infrastructure, because a smaller model that remembers beats a bigger one that forgets.
